speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about an injection you-- this is tape three, Joe Lipschutz. We were talking about an injection that you received in your thigh, and it got infected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it got infected. It got swollen, and so that my thigh was like a ball and to be operated on to remove the pus, the infection. And since the German doctor would not operate on me, I was transported to the ghetto. The colonel provided a horse, a buggy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] covered with-- with straw what I was laying on. And the soldier who was driving me to the-- into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken into the hospital in ghetto, the operation was performed. During the operation, that was-- the situation in ghetto and in ghetto hospital was very crude. The operation was performed, and during the operation I woke up. My-- my wrists were tied to the operating table, and I sit up and looked at all the blood on my thigh and I started saying, what are you doing to me? And the doctor, Doctor Lefkovich, who operated on me, just moved off and hit me in my face with his elbow, and I was-- they put the mask on me, and I was down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually they finished the operation. I wound up in the-- in hospital. This was already March, 1943. March 1943 was the time when the ghetto was liquidated, and the concentration camp in Plaszow was initiated, and they actually moved the people-- excuse me-- moved the people from ghetto to the concentration camp. In the process, they killed many in the ghetto before they moved them. And, of course, all the people who were in the hospital died by injecting or taking poison. And I was in that hospital. Since I had contacts-- people were-- people were two and sometimes three to a bed because there was no room. But since I had contacts, I had one bed. I was alone by myself in the bed, and my bed was close to the entrance door because this is where this smell was the least. I couldn't stand the smell of the hospital, all the pus and everything. Unfortunately, is proved not to be a big-- big deal, a big bargain. One night they-- they-- the Germans caught somebody outside the ghetto, and he was beaten, and he was taken to the hospital in the ghetto and guarded by Jewish policeman. And since my bed was close to the entrance door, it was also close to the bathroom. It was just next door to the-- to the door at which I was laying. And the man who was under arrest and guarded by the [INAUDIBLE] needed to go to the bathroom. He went to the bathroom and he escaped through the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: The following morning SS came in with guns drawn, and looked around, and I was the closest to the door. And the-- when they pull the gun he says, did you help him to escape? I uncovered-- my sheet, whatever I was covered with, and I had a bandage that big. And I said in German-- I spoke German well at that time-- I said, I cannot get out of bed, how could I have helped him? And he put the gun back in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow my words were adequate to save my life, because at that same time they shoot few people right there. They were just as innocent as I was. Nobody helped the guy, but they had to-- I don't know, I guess they-- they had to show that they did something. That they didn't allow the case just to disappear, get away without punishment. Somebody had to be punished. Fortunately, it wasn't-- it was not I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you eventually leave the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when they found out, in the airport, that the ghetto is going to be liquidated within a week, the colonel sent back the buggy, and the horse, and the buggy, and the soldier with the straw, and I was taken back to the airport. And within the next two, three days-- actually, two days later, all the people in the hospital were distributed poison, and they poisoned themselves because they were to be killed by the SS, not transported to the-- to the Plaszow camp. Eventually, I was in the airport, and recovered, and I continued my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other jobs besides the furrier work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at the airport. Well-- well, once in a while when they needed some people who-- for a job which had no specific designation, there were no people to do it, they looked through the group of people or group of Jews, and the picked some. All right, today you will be shoveling coal. But it was not my assignment. My assignment was to the fur shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with your brother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still with my brother at this time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the living conditions-- you were in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- lived in barracks. We had bunk beds, not-- not shelves like they do in concentration camps. We were given decent amount of food, and by comparison were doing well. One day the SS came in and decided to paint whatever we were wearing-- we had only one set of clothing-- whatever we were wearing they decided to paint stripes on. So you were wearing a jacket and a pair of pants, and they painted yellow stripes on it so that you would be recognized if you escape. And I believe they, at the same time, gave us the numbers. I'm not sure that this is when I got the number, or whether I got the number after, eventually, I later arrived in Plaszow. I don't know for sure. My number was 69,187. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This-- from August, 1942, till approximately June, 1943, we were all-- all who were assigned to the airport labor camp-- concentration camp, whichever you want-- were doing well. By June or July, 1943, the SS arrived, went through the list of people who the airport has employed, and decided who can stay and who will be taken immediately to concentration Plaszow. About 50% of the people were taken away. My brother, I, and most people who I was associated with stayed behind. We left-- we were still in the-- at the airport, and we were there till December, 1943. In December, 1943, they liquidated the airport, the airport facility, and we were all moved to Plaszow concentration camp. There I started working again in the fur shop, where they were repairing fur coats for the eastern front soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they move you from one-- how did they move you from--

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck. By truck, under guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far away was Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would guess this was-- we drove 10, 12 kilometers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And now-- now we were in Plaszow, and we were-- and our uniforms were changed into striped suits, and we were bonafide concentration camp inhabitants-- prisoners. And what happened there-- of course there was, as-- as people maybe do know, every morning there was a count of everybody present. And every evening was counted by the present, and you were allowed to go back to your living quarters, which was shelves on which people lived-- on shelves. Again, having contacts with various people in power, I was assigned a third floor shelf at the end. A corner-- I got a corner house, which was a tremendous advantage at that time. And I was also able to organize extra piece of bread, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes even an extra can of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was the position in your-- of your-- where you were laying an advantage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody was above you, and also nobody was next to you on one side. This was the best you could get. There was always-- I was on the right hand side corner on the top floor. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This pretty much lasted till August, 1944. In August, 1944, the Germans accused some of the commanders-- Jewish commanders of the Jewish police within the ghetto of trying to escape, and they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is within the camp. This is the Plaszow camp, not the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I said already, in December '43 we were moved to Plaszow camp. It was Plaszow camp. At that moment, a boy, seven years old, whose parents were shot, came running from another department to the fur shop, and he was a cousin of my-- of my deceased wife. He-- from that time on, my deceased wife-- we were not married at the time, she was just my girlfriend-- and I were taking care of him. He lived next to my shelf. He lived on the-- he live on the shelf next to me, and we lived that way till November, 1944. Sometime in September, 1944, the Germans decided that the Russians are coming close, and they going-- they're going to defend the camp. Maybe it was August, 1944. And they decided to dig machine gun trenches and thing like that to defend the positions, and I was assigned to dig the trenches. There were-- each group consisted of three people with wheelbarrow, a shovel, and a pick. Each one of us operated one of them. One wheeled the wheelbarrow, one wield the shovel, one was the-- hit the pick. And we switched around, and we had to dig machine gun emplacements. We were very tired, and we sit down on the wheelbarrow. We were sitting there for a while, and suddenly, from nowhere, a German SS man showed up and caught us sitting on the wheelbarrow. He stood us up at the edge of the ditch that we have dug, put on white gloves, unbuckled his revolver, and started hitting us over the face-- [? on ?] the face. And each time he hit us, we fell into the ditch. Up, up, up you go. And we had to come up, and this was going on for quite awhile. He hit us. We went down to the-- to the ditch. We came up, he hit us again, and down to the-- to the ditch. We were sure that he was going to shoot us, because he had his revolver already prepared for that. But for whatever reason, unknown to me, he took off his gloves, zippered his-- zippered or snapped back his revolver and walked away. He took our-- our numbers, however, and he reported us to-- for penalty because he caught us. The penalty consisted of-- of three days wheeling a wheelbarrow in a situation which I have to describe. They pumped out water from a lake, which was within the camp, and the bottom of the-- of the lake was all mud. And they constructed two, about 12 or 14-inch-wide boards going downhill into the lake, and we had to run with wheelbarrows downhill. Somebody loaded the wheelbarrows with mud, and we had to take another route on another board uphill and dump the mud. And whoever fell was down there. They wouldn't let you come up. And this took for three-- this took time. This took three days. After three days, I was done with the penalty. This was one of the horrible situations, because dozens and dozens of people fell in, never to show up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also telling a story about the seven-year-old boy that ran to your furrier where-- when you were working.

speaker: interviewee
text: This I will have to backtrack, because really I was not present. I did not see him there. I'm going with my story, and then I will backtrack to that. About a month later, which would be probably towards the end of September, 1944, the Germans supposedly got an order to evacuate the camp and move all the prisoners, the Jews, to other camps. But before they do that, they were supposed to destroy any evidence of their killing. The evidence of the killing was in a ravine, all-- all within the camp-- within camp itself, where they shot many people, who fell into the ravine. They just put dirt and some disinfectant over them, and shot the next group, and they were laying right on top of the other. And eventually, that thing was buried with dirt, and it was a mass grave. But when the order came to evacuate, and destroy the evidence of their action, they decided to un-dig the people. And we had to work in un-digging these corpses, and they were burning the corpses. I don't wish to refer to the but this is all shown there. In the process, they were examining-- some Jews were assigned to do that-- examining people's teeth, and pull gold teeth, and bury the-- bury the corpses otherwise. Whether they finished the job totally or not, I don't know. I worked there for two or three days, and I was free. Everybody in the camp was assigned a date at which he has to work there. I believe I was there three times. Eventually, since the camp was to be evacuated, rumors started going on that people are going to go to Gusen, people are going to go to Mauthausen, people are going to go to many other camps-- I don't remember them all by name-- and that some people will be going with Schindler to Czechoslovakia to his munition factory. But these people are being-- the list for these people is being prepared at that time, and Schindler will decide who are the people that he will take with him. While the list was being prepared, I was looking for ways to get on that list-- for my sister, my brother, and myself. And I did put all three of us on the list through contacts of my friends, even though it was very difficult because I was not at Schindler's-- none of us were at Schindler's originally, and these people had the priority. There were 300 of them, but he wanted 1,100.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were-- what people do not realize, people still had some valuables with them. I didn't have any. The valuables were diamonds, which they put into their molar teeth. When they realized that the ghetto was going to be liquidated, many people had their molar teeth drilled out, put diamonds in them, and filled. And this was the means by which people got on the list. They removed the diamonds from their teeth, and they paid up to get on the list. Now, these people are in Plaszow right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know about Schindler? How did you know even who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about him. We knew that Schindler has a site camp like the airport camp. There was another one was a-- where they used to make cables and wires. There were three outside camps, which eventually were-- well, they were all under jurisdiction of Plaszow and of it's commandant. At any rate, we all three got on the list. To make this story short, unfortunately, people who had the money paid enough money that my sister my brother were taken off the list, and I was-- I remained. This is why I wound up at Schindler's, and they did not. They were taken-- sometime in October my sister was taken to Stutthof, where she, unfortunately, was killed, and my brother was taken to Gusen, in Austria, where he, unfortunately, was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when people were giving-- giving their valuables, who was actually getting-- who was keeping the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know. I wasn't privileged enough to know what is happening behind the scenes. What I heard later-- but this would be more rumors or hearsay than-- than-- than real knowledge-- that some of that money went to Schindler. Some of that money and the diamonds went to the-- the Jewish people who were in charge of preparing the list for Schindler. One of them, possibly still alive, is, I think, in Argentina. I know that through the information received through the New Cracow Friendship Society of-- in New York, of which I am a member. Now to return to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] time, which we mentioned, that the boy was hidden in the latrine. This happened during May of 1943, when the Germans have decided that the concentration camp Plaszow will not have any children. And they assembled all the children and evacuated them, but few of them managed to escape and hide within the camp. One of them, who is my cousin, managed to get into the latrine and submerge himself up to his neck in the dirt, and stayed there till the children on the trucks were removed from the camp. Then he returned, and we managed to hide him from the Germans until he was evacuated from Plaszow. Actually, he managed to go with me to-- to Schindler's. But the Schindler's-- the-- the SS commandant at Schindler's camp refused to keep children. Therefore, he sent out all the children to Auschwitz. So he was only about two or three weeks at Schindler's. At that point, he was removed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you're-- you're on the-- you've been put on the list. Plaszow has been liquidated. What happens next for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me? I eventually was-- in the beginning of November, or middle of November, was-- the people on the list were assembled on a plaza, taken, on-- put on a train, and transported to a concentration camp, Gross-Rosen, which is in southeastern Germany, where we stayed for several days. It was a horrible camp. But because we were on the list, and because Schindler has made arrangements, to us it was only a transit camp. We spent a few days. We were shaven-- I mean, our heads were shaven. And our uniforms were taken away-- I mean, the-- the striped uniforms taken away, and we were going through a shower room, and come out through the other end, and gave-- given new uniforms. So nobody could really smuggle anything with him. As a matter of fact, I had hidden-- and you'll have to pardon me, my word-- I had hidden a $50 bill in my rectum, and a $10 gold piece taped to my foot, under my foot. When I was shaven there by these people-- they were prisoners, German and Polish prisoners shaving us-- they removed the $50 bill from my rectum, and they took it away. But they did not see the $10 gold piece, and I managed to get that through the shower and on-- into the-- into Schindler's camp. This $10 piece was to do some good for me, and then maybe a little bit, but not very much. Eventually, this $10 piece I gave to a person who was a-- Schindler's confidant. I was not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I made a deal with him that Schindler would give me an extra bread every week, one bread, for as long as we are in the camp, for that $10 gold piece. I couldn't break a piece to give him a piece. I couldn't buy piecemeal, I had to buy wholesale. And I said, OK, this is a good deal, but I want to see Mr. Schindler receiving that $10 gold piece. When I give it to you, I said to the guy, how do I know what you're gonna do with it? So he said he will talk with Mr. Schindler to see whether he will agree for me to see that. I said, that's fine. I will give it to you when Mr. Schindler agrees. Mr. Schindler agreed, and he came-- walked me to Mr. Schindler, and he handed Mr. Schindler that 10-- $10 gold piece. This ascertained to me that Mr. Schindler had received that $10 gold piece. Was this the first time you actually were face-to-face with--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first time that I was face-to-face with Schindler on-- in-- in this kind of situation. I faced him as a group-- in a group many times. He was in front of a group of people talking to us, but he never called me by name, or pointed me out, or whatever. I was a-- I was a nobody. But I was an electrician. When we came to Schindler's factory there, we found out that this is a rather large complex of red brick buildings which, originally, was a Jewish factory of materials-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what do you call materials?

speaker: interviewer
text: Fabric?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fabrics, fabrics, that's right. Fabrics and wool-- woolen things, and we have to empty that factory from these things, and prepare the site to receive the machinery which Schindler was to ship in, which he eventually did-- was-- which he eventually did. And the electrical department, of which I became an electrician, was divided into two people. The more experienced people were to work inside to wire the machinery, and the less experienced people were to work outside to build electric fence. And, of course, I was not experienced, so I was building the electric fence. During the months of December and January-- December '44 and January '45, I was actually working outside in snow and cold, building an electric fence. There we were under guards of SS, and one day I managed to organize several potatoes, and I baked the potatoes just simply making fire, burying the potatoes, and burning-- and baking them on the ground. And I was caught doing it, and the SS men beat me so badly that I lost my teeth, many of them. I lost my hearing. For seven or eight days, I didn't hear at all. Now I wear two hearing aids. Eventually my hearing returned, but it was damaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's switch tapes, so we can get into the whole--
